coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence probe based on carbon dots for determination of dopamine utilizing its self-polymerization
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xin
Yu, Wei
Mu, Xiaowei
Zhang, Wei
Wang, Xinghua
Gu, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: A rapid and sensitive strategy for sensing dopamine (DA) was proposed based on the fluorescence quenching effects of polydopamine (PDA) on carbon dots (CDs). The green-emission fluorescence CDs were synthesized via a facile one-pot hydrothermal approach by employing p-phenylenediamine and ethanol as reagents. In alkaline environments, DA would polymerize to form PDA on surface of CDs, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of the detection system owing to the effects of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FERT) and inner filter effect (IFE). The proposed fluorescence probe exhibits good selectivity and sensitivity to DA in the concentration range of 0.1–15 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 37 nM. Results of detecting DA in serum samples indicate the broad potential of the proposed strategy for future application in diagnosis of DA-related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine
Polydopamine
Fluorescence quenching
Carbon dots
Serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence excitation (A) and emission (B) spectra of CDs, UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (C), DA (D) and PDA (E). (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of hybrids containing CDs and PDA with different original concentration of DA (0–80 μM). Inset: the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence of DA with a series of concentrations. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelength (310 nm–400 nm). (d) Variation of fluorescence intensity of CDs under continuous illumination for 1 h (λex = 370 nm, I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities emitted from CDs before and after continuous illumination, respectively).

(a) TEM image of CDs. (b) TEM image of a hybrid containing CDs and PDA. (c) FT-IR spectra of CDs, hybrid containing CDs and PDA, PDA. (d) XPS spectra of CDs and a hybrid containing CDs and PDA. (e) Fluorescence decay profile of CDs. (f) Fluorescence decay profile of a hybrid containing CDs and PDA.

(a) Effect of environmental pH level on fluorescence emission intensity of CDs. (b) Effect of environmental pH level on self-polymerization of DA. (c) Effect of incubation time on self-polymerization of DA. (d) Effect of incubation temperature on self-polymerization of DA.

(a) Effects of common biomolecules and inorganic ions on fluorescence emission intensity of CDs. (b) Effects of common biomolecules and inorganic ions on fluorescence emission intensity of the hybrid containing CDs and PDA. Concentration of each biomolecule or inorganic ion, 100 μM.
(a) Stern-Volmer plot of fluorescence quenching by dopamine for CDs. (b) Calibration line of for quantification of DA.
Schematic illustration of mechanism for sensing DA.
Comparison of various reported methods for DA detection.
Recoveries of DA in spiked human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A fluorescence probe based on carbon dots for determination of dopamine utilizing its self-polymerization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Hand Surgery, China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University, 126 Xiantai Street, Changchun 130033, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Hand Surgery, China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University, 126 Xiantai Street, Changchun 130033, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) was synthesized from p-phenylenediamine and ethanol by a hydrothermal method, and was used as the fluorescence indicator for detecting dopamine in human serum samples via inner filter effect (IFE) and Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and polydopamine (PDA) generated by the self-polymerization of DA.

A rapid and sensitive strategy for sensing dopamine (DA) was proposed based on the fluorescence quenching effects of polydopamine (PDA) on carbon dots (CDs). The green-emission fluorescence CDs were synthesized via a facile one-pot hydrothermal approach by employing p-phenylenediamine and ethanol as reagents. In alkaline environments, DA would polymerize to form PDA on surface of CDs, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of the detection system owing to the effects of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FERT) and inner filter effect (IFE). The proposed fluorescence probe exhibits good selectivity and sensitivity to DA in the concentration range of 0.1–15 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 37 nM. Results of detecting DA in serum samples indicate the broad potential of the proposed strategy for future application in diagnosis of DA-related diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA), one of the most significant catecholamine neurotransmitters in mammals’ central nervous system, plays a crucial role in the regulation of nervous activity, and affects the behavior, emotion, learning ability and memory of individual greatly [1–5]. Lack of DA in human body will lead to various diseases, such as schizophrenia, depression and Parkinson's disease [6–9]. Therefore, it will provide great benefit to develop a facile and precise approach for detecting DA in tissues or fluids of human body for monitoring physiological processes and diagnosis of neurological diseases. Currently, various strategies to determine DA have been developed by using high performance liquid chromatography [10,11], enzymatic techniques [12,13], and electrochemical means [14,15]. However, some disadvantages of these strategies, including requirements of expensive instruments, time-consuming sample pretreatment procedures or cumbersome post-processing operations, limit their practical applications. Fortunately, strategies based on fluorometry will facilitate the detection of DA in biosamples by providing more cost-efficiency and maneuverability [16,17].

As is known to all, DA will polymerize to form polydopamine (PDA) films on the surfaces of almost all kinds of materials under alkaline conditions [18,19]. With its remarkable physicochemical properties, PDA has been broadly applied in material coating, drug delivery, and immunoassay [19–22]. However, strategies by utilizing the self-polymerization of DA to form PDA for sensing DA in biosamples via fluorometry have been rarely reported up to now.

In recent years, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensively attention due to their facile synthetic procedures, excellent optical performance and good biocompatibility [23,24], and have been broadly applied in bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics [23,25–27]. Raw CDs can be obtained via hydrothermal carbonization, laser ablation, microwave-assisted pyrolysis, electrochemical exfoliation, acid dehydration or ultrasonic treatment [28–33].

In this work, we synthesized a new type of nitrogen-doped CDs by using p-phenylenediamine and ethanol as precursors via a facile hydrothermal approach. Then, we proposed a novel strategy for detecting DA by utilizing the self-polymerization of DA on the surface of CDs and the consequent fluorescence quenching on CDs by PDA. The mechanism of the fluorescence quenching effect was investigated, and was ascribe to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) between CDs and PDA. The proposed strategy was furtherly applied to the determination of DA in human serum samples and demonstrated its potential in clinical diagnosis.

P-phenylenediamine was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Dopamine (DA) was obtained from J&K Scientific (Beijing, China). Other reagents were analytical reagent grade and were used without further purification or treatment. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water supplied by a Milli-Q water purification system (18.2 MΩ cm−1). Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 9.0) consisting of 50 mM Tris-(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane and 25 mM HCl was used for the self-polymerization of DA. A stock DA solution at the concentration of 10 mM was prepared in deionized water. A human serum sample was provided by China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University.

All fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopic measurements in this study were operated with a F-7000 spectrofluorophotometer (HITACHI Co., ltd., Japan) and a UV-1700 UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu Co., ltd., Japan), respectively. The morphological investigation of CDs was performed by a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL ltd., Japan). Infrared (IR) spectra of CDs, PDA and the hybrid containing both CDs and PDA in wavenumber region of 4000–400 cm−1 were recorded on a Nicolet AVATAR 360 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed by using an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA) operated at 250 W.

The synthetic procedure of CDs was similar to that reported in our previous work [26]. Firstly, 0.1 g of p-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol sufficiently. Then the solution was heated in a 30-mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave at 180℃ for 12 h. Subsequently, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the product was purified by a silica gel column using a mixture of dichloromethane and methanol as mobile phase. The resulting CDs were dried and redispersed in ethanol for future use without further modification.

The hybrid containing CDs and PDA for UV–vis, FT-IR and XPS characterizations was prepared via adding 500 μL of the synthesized CDs into 500 μL of DA solution (100 μM, pH = 9.00) followed by incubation at room temperature for 90 min.

Standard DA solutions were obtained by diluting the stock solution in deionized water before use. Detecting procedure was performed as follows: Firstly, 10 μL of fresh DA aqueous solution at concentrations ranging from 0 to 15 μM or 10 μL of real sample solution, 490 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 9), and 500 μL of CDs were mixed thoroughly. Then, the mixtures were incubated at 60℃ for 90 min to ensure the polymerization of DA, which would result in the fluorescence quenching of CDs. Finally, fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were collected at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The fluorescence intensities at the maximum emission wavelength of 500 nm were employed for building calibration curve and calculating concentrations of DA in real samples.

For optimizing experiment conditions, evaluating interference induced by interferents, and quantifying DA in real samples, the ratio of fluorescence decrease (Rfd) was calculated to indicate the efficiency of fluorescence quenching according to the equation: Rfd = (I0 - I) / I0 , where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities emitted from CDs in a blank sample and a standard (or real) sample, respectively.

To examine the optical properties of CDs, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were collected and profiled (Fig. 1 a). The fluorescence maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs are 370 nm (spectrum A) and 500 nm (spectrum B), respectively. In the absorption spectrum of CDs (spectrum C), π - π* transition of CC bond [34] and n - π* transition of CN bond [35,36] are separately emerged at 270 nm and 412 nm. Spectra D and E are individually the UV–vis absorption spectra of DA solution (100 μM) under neutral (pH = 7.00) and alkaline (pH = 9.00) conditions. Compared with spectrum D, a red shift of the absorption peak can be observed in spectrum E, which would be ascribed to the generation of PDA via the self-polymerization of DA in alkaline environments. In addition, spectrum E exhibits significant absorption in a wide wavelength range in which both the fluorescence maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs are covered, indicating that the fluorescence of CDs would be quenched efficiently by PDA.

Moreover, with the increase of the original DA concentration, the absorption of the final hybrid containing both CDs and PDA was enhanced, while the fluorescence emitted from CDs was decreased obviously (Fig. 1b), which reveals that the absorption-induced decrement of fluorescence intensity can be used to indicate the amount of both PDA and the original DA.

As pictured in Fig. 1c, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of CDs varied little when the excitation wavelength was changed in the range of 310 nm–400 nm, indicating the excitation-independent emission property of CDs. Testing results of fluorescence resistance to photo-bleaching under continuous UV illumination at 370 nm (Fig. 1d) suggest the good photo-stability of CDs.

According to the results of previous studies [18,19], PDA generated by the self-polymerization of DA might adhere onto the surface of the nanoparticles dispersed in the reaction solution. In this work, morphologies and diameters of CDs before and after the generation of PDA were distinguished by TEM and its auxiliary software (Nanomeasure). The diameter of original CDs ranged from 1.1 nm to 2.9 nm, with an average size about 1.7 nm (Fig. 2 a). After the generation of PDA, the average diameter of particles increased to approximately 3.2 nm (Fig. 2b), which confirmed the formation of PDA-wrapped CDs.

FT-IR spectroscopic analysis was executed to identify the formation of the hybrid containing CDs and PDA (Fig. 2c). For original CDs, the characteristic absorption bands indicate the stretching vibrations of OH at 3299 cm−1, CH at 2971 cm−1, CC and NO in aromatic ring at 1377 cm−1, CC, CN and CO at 1046 cm−1, and bending vibration between CH and para-substitution CN in aromatic ring at 882 cm−1. For PDA, the absorption bands at 3343, 3222, 3037 cm−1 reveal the existence of NH, OH and CH bonds. The bands at 1616, 1500, 1287, and 1178 cm−1 indicate stretching vibrations of CC, CO, and CN groups. The bands at 814 and 601 cm−1 correspond to bending vibrations of aromatic CH. For the hybrid of CDs and PDA, in addition to the wavenumber ranges of 2492–3353 cm−1 and 632–912 cm−1 containing the overlapping bands of CDs and PDA, the absorption bands at 1038 and 1398 cm−1 are assigned to CDs, the bands at 1159, 1287, 1472, and 1598 cm−1 are attributed to PDA.

XPS was used to further confirm the formation of PDA. Detailed elemental analyses of the CDs and the hybrid of CDs and PDA are displayed in Fig. 2d. Compared with that of the original CDs, the atomic percentage of N in the hybrid increased obviously from 6.04 % to 9.67 %, while the percentage of C decreased from 66.08 % to 61.12 %, which were caused by the generation of PDA.

Due to the obvious overlaps between the absorption band of PDA and the fluorescence excitation and emission bands of CDs (Fig. 1a), there are two possible approaches, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FERT) and inner filter effect (IFE), which may induce fluorescence quenching of CDs in this work. FRET is a non-radiative and distance-dependent approach for energy transfer, by which the energy of a fluorophore at electronic excited singlet state (donor) can be transferred to a coupled molecule or group at ground state (acceptor) spontaneously, if the distance between the donor and the acceptor is less than 10 nm. And consequently, the fluorescence of the donor will be quenched. The fluorescent lifetime of the donor, which is determined mainly by the keeping time of its excited singlet state, will also be shortened [37–39]. IFE is an effect which would occur in a mixed chemical system when the absorption band of an absorber overlaps with the fluorescence excitation or emission bands of a fluorophore [40,41]. Compared with FRET, IFE will not cause the fluorescent lifetime of the corresponding fluorophore to be changed. Results of fluorescent lifetime (Fig. 2e, f) and zeta potential measurements for CDs in the absence and presence of PDA show that the generation of PDA leads to both a fluorescent lifetime decrease of 0.78 ns (∼9.56 %) from 8.1625 ns to 7.3797 ns and a zeta potential change from −2.69 mV to 8.19 mV, suggesting the FRET occurs due to the coating of PDA on the surface of CDs via electrostatic binding. Meanwhile, the dispersive PDA in the detection system would also absorb of fluorescence excitation and emission light of CDs. Therefore, it can be concluded that both the FRET and the IFE contribute to the fluorescence quenching of CDs (Scheme 1 ).

To attain good analytical performance, several experimental parameters which may greatly influence the accuracy, sensitivity and efficiency of the proposed strategy for detecting DA, including the environmental pH level for both fluorescence emissions of CDs and self-polymerization of DA, incubation time and temperature for polymerization reaction of DA, were investigated and optimized. As shown in Fig. 3 a, environmental pH level ranging from 6 to 10.5 has a very slight influence on the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs. However, alkaline environments greatly improved the Rfd of the detection system (Fig. 3b), since the oxidative self-polymerization of DA would occur efficiently in alkaline media [13,37]. Therefore, an environmental pH level of 9.0 was selected for the following experiments.

It can be seen from Fig. 3c and d that incubation time and temperature have remarkable influence on the self-polymerization of DA. After incubation for 90 min at 60 ℃, the Rfd value tends to stable. Based on the above results, time and temperature for incubation of the detection system before fluorescence analysis were set at 90 min and 60 ℃, respectively.

Selectivity of the proposed strategy towards sensing DA was evaluated by testing the influence of chemicals and biomolecules commonly coexisted in human serum on the determination of DA. Solutions of biomolecules including l -cysteine ( l -Cys), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), homocysteine (Hcy), histidine (His), and inorganic ions including Ca2+, K+, Na+, were examined solely after the addition of Tris-HCl buffer solutions and CDs (Fig. 4 a). It can be observed that these biomolecules and ions induced very slight fluorescence variations of CDs. Furthermore, comparative experiments were carried out by using DA coexisting with the above interferents as samples. Results (Fig. 4b) indicate that these substances have little influence on the detection of DA.

The Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 5 a) exhibited a linear correlation between fluorescence quenching efficiencies and the concentrations of dopamine from 0.1 to 15 μM. Where I0 and I refer to the fluorescence emission intensities in a blank sample and dopamine sample, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5b, calibration line for quantification of DA was constructed by plotting the Rfd value versus the concentration of DA, which demonstrates a good linearity in the concentration range of 0.1–15 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 37 nM, which was comparable with previous reports (Table 1 ). Although mass spectrometry or electrochemical methods provided lower LOD values, detecting dopamine with fluorescence spectroscopy has the potential to be applied in cellular imaging.

Detections of DA in spiked human serum samples were performed to evaluate the applicability of the proposed strategy. Before analysis, serum samples were diluted 100-fold with Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 9) solution. The fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of the diluted serum were collected (Fig. S1), which indicate that the matrix effect of the sample would influence little on the detection of DA. Results (Table 2 ) showed that good recoveries ranging from 94.0 % to 106.7 % were obtained, demonstrating the excellent applicability of the proposed strategy for determination of DA in complex biosamples.

We propose a novel strategy for the quantitative detection of DA based on the fluorescence quenching effects of PDA generated by the self-polymerization of DA under alkaline conditions on CDs. The quenching mechanism was characterized by means of TEM, FT-IR, XPS, zeta-potential and fluorescence lifetime measurements, and was confirmed as the combination of FRET and IFE. Results for determination of DA in standard and real human serum samples demonstrate the high sensitivity and selectivity as well as good accuracy of the proposed strategy, also indicate its potential application in future clinical diagnose or research of drug metabolism and pharmacokinetics.

Xin Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Wei Yu: . Xiaowei Mu: . Wei Zhang: . Xinghua Wang: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Qiang Gu: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Research Project of the Education Department of Jilin Province (No. JJKH20211187KJ and JJKH20200944KJ), and the Industrial Technology Research Project of the Development and Reform Commission of Jilin Province (No. 2021C042-4).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122112.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
